# Chapter 12

[q1k7m2z9] The invitation arrived in the thin hour before dawn when the solar batteries were still deciding if they wanted to be alive. Flynn was on the roof with a wrench and a length of copper wire, listening to the compound wake in the dark. The desert was cold enough to make your teeth hurt. The stars looked like holes punched through a sheet of black. His implant pinged and tried to sell him electrolyte gum. He ignored it and opened the message anyway. A file. An emblem: a red pepper curled like a hook. Coordinates in Sonora. A date. A countdown.

[n4v8c0p3] Cage was already in the yard when Flynn climbed down, barefoot in the dust like he had never slept. He had the little screen in his hand, holding it out to the others as if it were a piece of meat. Birdie moved close, eyes wide, hair sticking up. Wrench stayed by the workbench with his arms crossed. Diesel came last, wiping grease from her knuckles, looking at the screen like it might bite. Cage didn’t smile. He didn’t need to. The air around him tightened.

[t8h2x6d1] The video auto-played with the volume low, then rose to full without asking, like everything did now. A man sat in a white room with glass behind him and a strip of green that could’ve been plants or a screen pretending to be plants. Older. Hair silver. Skin browned by sun and money. His Spanish was careful and his English was better than Flynn’s. He spoke about roads that didn’t belong to anyone, about engines that burned what the world wanted buried, about men who remembered what fear tasted like. He said La Carrera del Chile Rojo was not a race. It was a confession. The screen tried to overlay an ad for a mindfulness patch and Cage slapped it away.

[y5r9b1k0] Behind Don Castellanos the glass showed a field that didn’t make sense in the desert. Rows of chilli plants in hydroponic troughs, bright green against the dirt, a country of leaves. The camera drifted once, almost on purpose, to show the fortress of his compound and the men with rifles who weren’t pretending to be anything else. Flynn felt the old highway maps in his head, the way borders used to mean something. Now they were suggestions. If you had a fast car and enough gas you could cross anything.

[m0s7j3a8] Then the prize appeared like an altar. A case of bills stacked tight, the kind of money you only saw in movies from before the pods. And beside it, under a cloth that slid away as if obeying a prayer, a car the color of dried blood. Long hood. Wide shoulders. Chrome that looked like teeth. Castellanos called it a ’71 Hemi ’Cuda like the name itself was a spell. He said it had belonged to his father. He said it would belong to the one who finished first, running. He didn’t say alive. He didn’t have to.

[u2c5w9e4] Cage stared at the screen as if he could climb into it. Flynn watched his face and tried to find the boy who’d once been excited about engines the way Flynn was. All he saw was hunger. The kind that didn’t end. Birdie was breathing fast. Diesel’s eyes went to the Shelby where it sat under a tarp, quiet and perfect, like an animal that had been drugged. Wrench looked away, and when he looked back it was with the expression of a man reading a weather report that promised hail.

[g7p1f6l2] They went into the shop because talking outside made the words float away. The fans kicked on, pushing hot air in circles. Diesel spread out a stained paper map and a cheap screen with route options. Mexico was a long way when you had to avoid cameras, keep to broken roads, move fuel in plastic jugs without attracting anyone who asked questions. They needed tires and spare parts and brake pads and filters and water and medicine and money for the people who let you pass. Flynn had been thinking of the race like a dream. Diesel made it math.

[b3z8n0q5] She wrote numbers in grease pencil on the side of a refrigerator that didn’t cool anymore. Two hundred thousand, she said, and that was if nothing went wrong. Fuel alone was a fortune when you couldn’t buy it honest. The black-market gas Birdie had found came in blue drums that smelled like chemical death, cut with whatever they could get. They could run vegetable oil in the rat-rods but not in the Shelby. The Shelby wanted what it had always wanted. Old dinosaur blood. Wrench rubbed his jaw. Birdie said they could do it cheaper. Diesel didn’t answer him.

[k9d2s4h7] Cage listened with his head tilted like he was bored. When Diesel finished, he nodded once, as if she’d just told him the time. “We’ve got twenty,” he said. He didn’t say dollars, because twenty thousand sounded too small to admit out loud. He said it like a challenge. Flynn felt that sickness in his throat again, the feeling that something was moving under the floor. Diesel looked at Flynn and then away. Wrench cleared his throat, like that could clean the air.

[r6x1m8v3] “We sell,” Birdie said, quick. He was already halfway into the fantasy. “We sell parts. We sell the rat-rod. We sell whatever.” Cage shook his head. “Not the Shelby,” Birdie added, like he was afraid Cage would think it. Cage’s eyes were bright and empty. He leaned on the workbench, palms flat, like a preacher about to give the real sermon. He said the world still had money. It just wasn’t in their hands. He said there were men in Phoenix who kept cars like pets, fed them climate-controlled air, never let them run.

[a4j0c7t5] He said the name like he’d been tasting it for days. Khoury. The billionaire with the glass garage. The same place they’d bled a guard on polished concrete. Flynn saw that man’s face again, the surprise, the way his body had folded under Cage’s fists like it was made of cloth. Diesel’s jaw tightened. Wrench’s eyes went to the door as if he could already hear sirens. Cage said Khoury had fifty cars. “We take three more,” Cage said. “We sell two. We keep the one we need. We go south with a convoy.”

[p8e3y6u1] Diesel laughed once, sharp and humorless. “That’s not a heist. That’s a robbery.” Cage shrugged. “It’s redistribution.” He said it the way a politician would, the word clean and stupid. Diesel stepped closer to him. Her hands were still greasy. “We got lucky,” she said. “We got lucky and you turned it into a game. The news is watching. The city’s watching. You don’t rob a billionaire twice and walk away.” Cage looked at her like she was slow. “They don’t drive,” he said. “They don’t move. They sit in their pods and think the desert can’t touch them.”

[d1l9g2m4] Wrench finally spoke. His voice sounded like sand. “This is too far.” He didn’t explain, because they all knew where far was. Flynn felt the words inside him too, but they wouldn’t come out. Cage turned his head and regarded Wrench with a strange tenderness, like he remembered him as a kid with scraped knees. “Then don’t come,” Cage said. The shop went quiet except for the fan bearings whining. Birdie’s mouth opened and shut. Diesel stared at the floor. Flynn watched Wrench’s face and saw it break into something like grief.

[h5q7b0n8] Cage kept talking because silence was dangerous and he hated it. He said if they stole cars again, they couldn’t just sneak in and leave. Khoury would harden the place. Hire better guards. Put in military locks. Bring in drones. So you did it once, you did it right. Cage’s words sped up. You took the man himself. You took his access, his codes, his soft hands. You made him open the doors for you. You made him move money where you wanted it. “He’ll do it,” Cage said, calm again. “They always do. They love living more than anything.”

[s2w4r1k6] Diesel’s eyes lifted, flat and cold. “Kidnapping,” she said. Like naming a thing made it real and heavy. Cage nodded. “Temporary,” he said. “We don’t hurt him.” Flynn didn’t believe him and that made him angry at himself, because he still wanted to. Birdie swallowed and nodded too, as if his throat had locked around his own doubt. Wrench shook his head slowly, over and over, like a man at a funeral refusing to accept the body in front of him.

[f0u6p9x2] Cage smiled then, the first one Flynn had seen all day, and it was worse than the violence. “It’s not death,” Cage said. “That’s the point. Nobody dies anymore. You crack a rib, they print you a brace. You stop breathing, they plug you in and drag you back. The world did all this so nobody would have to pay for anything. So they could sin without consequence. So why should we be the only ones living like there are rules?” He looked at Flynn when he said it, like he was giving him a gift. Flynn felt the gift burning in his hands.

[v9m3a5c0] Outside, the sun climbed and the compound heated up fast, the metal walls turning into a stove. Flynn walked out to the Shelby and pulled the tarp back a corner. The paint caught the light and threw it back. A museum car wearing dust. He put his hand on the fender and felt nothing but cool metal. Diesel came up behind him and didn’t touch the car. “You don’t have to do this,” she said, low. Flynn didn’t answer. He could hear Birdie laughing in the shop at something Cage had said, the laughter too high, too eager.

[c8n1t7d9] They gathered again when the heat started to wobble the air. Diesel said she was out. She didn’t shout it. She said it like a mechanic calling a part dead. Cage nodded like he’d expected it. “Fine,” he said. “Stay here. Keep the lights on.” Diesel’s face didn’t change. “If you do this,” she said, “you’re not racers anymore. You’re predators.” Cage’s eyes narrowed. “We’ve always been predators,” he said. “You just liked the name when it was romantic.”

[z0k5h2s4] Flynn heard himself speak before he could stop it. “What about after?” he said. Cage turned to him, interested. “After we take him,” Flynn said, “after we take the money and the cars. He’ll call them. He’ll tell them where we are.” Cage’s expression softened, almost patient. “He’ll be alive,” Cage said. “He’ll be grateful. He’ll go back to his glass house and tell himself it was a nightmare.” Wrench made a sound in his throat like a cough. Diesel looked at Flynn as if she’d been waiting for him to stand up all morning.

[j3p7n1v8] Cage went back to the whiteboard like the question had been answered by his certainty. He drew boxes with a marker that was almost dry. Home. Garage. Transit. Desert. A dot where the safe house would be, some abandoned structure with a roof that still cast shade. Flynn watched the lines get made and felt how easy it was to turn a person into a diagram. Birdie leaned in, nodding at every arrow. Wrench stayed in the corner, not looking at the board, looking at the floor as if he was trying to see through it.

[e6d0k9r2] Diesel said, quiet, “You can’t rely on their systems to revive him if something happens.” She meant it as a warning and Cage heard it as an insult. He turned his head. “We’re not going to kill him,” he said. Diesel didn’t argue. She just held his eyes. Flynn understood that in her mind killing wasn’t the worst outcome. The worst was what came after. A man waking up with his wrists raw and his family’s faces burned into him and no place to put the fear. Revival didn’t stitch that up.

[w4h8s1m5] Cage tapped the screen again and let Castellanos’s message play from the beginning, the way a drunk replays a song. The older man’s voice filled the shop and made the place feel smaller. Confession. Freedom. Pain. The words made Birdie shiver. Flynn felt them too, the old pull toward some clean edge where you could stand and say you had chosen. Diesel’s mouth tightened as if she could taste the manipulation. Wrench’s shoulders rose, then dropped, as if he’d been holding his breath for years.

[x2c6a9t0] When the video ended the screen offered them a discount on desert sunblock. Cage laughed at that, a short bark, and the laugh did something ugly to Flynn’s stomach. He saw it then, the way Cage could use anything. A race, a sermon, a car, a boy. He could use Flynn. He already was. Flynn stepped back and felt the heat on his face like a hand. He thought of the guard on the floor, of the crash in the desert, of Diesel’s bruised knuckles, of Birdie’s worship. It felt like standing at the lip of a hole and realizing you’d been digging it yourself.

[m8v3a5c0] Cage stepped close enough that Flynn could smell the gasoline on him, old and sweet. “You’re thinking like a pod,” Cage said. “Like a citizen. Like somebody who believes the state can protect them. You’ve driven. You’ve felt it. You know the truth. The only law is consequence. The only thing that’s real is what your hands do.” He placed two fingers on Flynn’s chest, not hard, like marking him. “You in?” he asked.

[l7q2p8s1] Flynn looked down at his hands. The nails were rimmed with black. Small cuts across the knuckles, healed badly. He’d earned them in this place, under Diesel’s quiet instruction, under Cage’s stare. In the city his hands had only tapped glass. In the desert they made things run. The thought should have felt clean. Instead it felt like proof of ownership. He could feel the room waiting. He could feel Cage’s patience like a blade held still. Outside the shop the heat shimmered, the world turning white and harsh, like a camera overexposed. Flynn understood that if he said no he’d be alone again, walking the shoulder of some dead highway while pods slid past with their windows sealed.

[t7d9x2q1] Flynn looked at Birdie. The kid’s face was lit with devotion. Flynn looked at Diesel and saw a door closing. He looked at Wrench and saw a man already halfway gone. He looked at Cage and saw the road stretching straight into darkness, no exits, just speed. Flynn felt his mouth open and the word come out like a bolt dropping. “Yeah,” he said. Cage nodded once, satisfied, and the shop went back to breathing. Outside, the desert kept its quiet, indifferent as a judge.
